narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41.9% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 26.50, first-year decile ranking of 1st, third-year decile of 2nd, upper-middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is much higher at 37.27, and first-year deciles average around 6th, highlighting where your profile stands out. The three most influential factors in this decision were your undergraduate GPA, family income bracket, and first-year decile ranking. Your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive sway toward predicting a bar exam pass, as it aligns closely with the 3.24 average among successful students, helping to pull the odds in your favor. Similarly, your upper-middle family income bracket, which is common among bar passers (representing about 45% of them), also nudged the prediction toward success by matching patterns seen in those who succeed. However, your first-year decile ranking of 1st worked against you, pushing the model toward predicting failure since it falls well below the 6th decile average for those who passed, signaling early academic challenges that often correlate with bar struggles. Overall, while your GPA and background provided some reassurance, the low early decile tipped the balance toward rejection. Compared to typical admitted students—who often enter with stronger early rankings and higher test scores—your profile showed promise in later years and stability but was held back by that initial performance dip. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the passing average, strengthening your early academic record through additional coursework or recommendations, and reapplying next cycle with updated achievements. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas, and we're here to support your next steps.
